title: The Tough Brets

jokes.text: Opening rap song dissing every major rapper by name: 'Eminem is not very good / 50 Cent is not very good / Snoop Dogg is not very good...' followed by 'bass solo. Bass solo over.'
jokes.setup: Bret performs what sounds like it might be a hype rap, naming famous rappers
jokes.punchline: Every line is simply '[rapper name] is not very good' — the entire song is just a list of disses, then 'bass solo. Bass solo over.' deflates any remaining energy
jokes.explanation: The joke earns its score through perfect economy — the entire rap is just '[name] is not very good' repeated. The bathetic simplicity of the diss, combined with Bret's total sincerity, is quintessential Flight of the Conchords. 'Bass solo. Bass solo over.' is a masterful little button that deflates the moment completely. Character-specific: only these two could perform this with a straight face.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: eminem is not very good

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: 50 cent is not very good

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Snoop dogg is not very good

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: mos def is not very good

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: but the rhymenoceros is very very good...

jokes.text: Murray taking band meeting attendance: 'Uh, well, Jemaine? Present. Who else have we got here? Bret? Yup. And who else? Mu... mur Ray. Oh.'
jokes.setup: Murray runs roll call as if he doesn't know who's in the band
jokes.punchline: He has to slowly read his own name off the list and is mildly surprised to find himself on it
jokes.explanation: Beautifully Murray-specific moment. The joke works on multiple levels: he's incompetent enough to put himself on the attendance list, he struggles to read his own name, and then is mildly baffled by finding it there. The hesitation 'Mu... mur Ray. Oh.' is perfectly paced deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: bass solo. Bass solo over.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Greg's written this. He's put the Rs too far apart.'
jokes.setup: Murray is baffled by his own name on the attendance list
jokes.punchline: He blames Greg's handwriting for putting the Rs too far apart — as if 'MurRay' with separated Rs is the real issue, not that he forgot his own name
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation button on the previous joke. Murray deflects from his own absurdity by blaming Greg's letter spacing. Classic Murray: never acknowledging his own incompetence, always finding an external explanation.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Greg's written this. He's put the rs too far apart.

jokes.text: Texas/Lexus as rhyming words for the band to use: 'Item one: Texas, Lexus... two rhyming words. Do you think you could use those?' — 'No.' — 'Maybe.'
jokes.setup: Murray presents agenda items to the band as their manager
jokes.punchline: His first agenda item is two rhyming words he'd like them to incorporate, delivered with complete bureaucratic seriousness; Jemaine's flat 'No' and Bret's qualified 'Maybe' are perfect
jokes.explanation: Sharp bit of absurdist character comedy. Murray's complete sincerity about presenting 'Texas, Lexus' as a formal agenda item, and then logging Bret's 'Maybe' as an official response, captures the show's deadpan bureaucratic absurdism perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Texas, Lexus... two rhyming words. Do you think you could use those?

jokes.text: The band has been playing gigs in a library and complains: 'It's so hard to play there because everyone wants us to be quiet. They're shushing us constantly.' Murray: 'You can make sound, just make it so that people can't hear you.'
jokes.setup: The band complains that library patrons shush them during performances
jokes.punchline: Murray's solution — play, but make it inaudible — is delivered as if it's perfectly reasonable advice
jokes.explanation: Escalating absurdist logic. The premise (a band booked into a library) is already funny; Bret's complaint that people 'want us to be quiet' escalates it; Murray's Zen-like solution ('make sound people can't hear') is the logical endpoint of their collective obliviousness. Well-structured three-beat joke.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You can make sound, just make it so that people can't hear you.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I don't know what that means. Does that make any sense to you?' about the word 'dissing' in his own manager's notes.
jokes.setup: Murray reads his own notes about the gig
jokes.punchline: He doesn't know what 'dissing' means, even though he presumably wrote it down
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy establishing Murray's cluelessness about hip-hop culture. Not exceptional on its own but serves the episode's setup well.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, it was Snoop dogg.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I know he's a dog, Bret.

jokes.text: Murray: 'The only one I could make out was Snoopy. What's your problem with him?' Bret: 'No, it was Snoop Dogg.' Murray: 'I know he's a dog, Bret. I'm not totally in the dark ages. I do go out once in a while. He's lovable. Leave him alone.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to identify who Bret was dissing in the rap
jokes.punchline: He conflates Snoop Dogg with Snoopy the cartoon dog, then doubles down by insisting he knows Snoop Dogg is a dog (the cartoon kind), that he's 'lovable,' and that Bret should 'leave him alone'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's strongest dialogue joke. Murray's confusion of Snoop Dogg with Snoopy is already funny, but the escalation — 'I know he's a dog, Bret. I'm not totally in the dark ages' — is masterful. He takes the wrong interpretation and defends it with wounded dignity, as if Bret is the one being condescending. 'He's lovable. Leave him alone.' is perfectly economical and quotable. Could only be Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: He's lovable. Leave him alone.

jokes.text: Murray's intercom to Greg has been broken — Greg has been hearing everything Murray says about him all year. 'You're only supposed to hear when I have the button down. Can you hear that?' — 'It is broken, Murray.' — 'Broken? How long have you been hearing me?' — 'All year.'
jokes.setup: Murray discovers Greg can hear him through the intercom
jokes.punchline: 'All year' — delivered flatly by Greg, implying Murray has been disparaging Greg to his face all year without realizing it
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured slow-burn reveal. The comedy builds through the realization: Murray's private diss sessions have been completely public for a year. 'All year' is a perfectly economical punchline — maximum damage, minimum words. Greg's flat delivery makes it land harder. Strong structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: All year.

jokes.text: Murray, after realizing Greg heard him, immediately disparages Greg again: 'What's wrong with that guy? Useless. What does he do all day?' then asks 'You didn't hear that, did you, Greg?' — then celebrates: 'Good, so he didn't hear that last bit. That's how you diss someone.'
jokes.setup: Murray just learned Greg can hear everything through the broken intercom
jokes.punchline: He immediately disses Greg again, then is relieved when he thinks Greg didn't hear — not understanding the irony, and tutoring the boys on 'proper' dissing technique
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation on the intercom bit. Murray learns nothing from the revelation and immediately repeats the exact behavior, then presents it as wisdom: 'That's how you diss someone.' The audience knows Greg almost certainly heard, making Murray's self-congratulation maximally cringeworthy. Pure Murray character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: What's wrong with that guy? Useless. What does he do all day?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You didn't hear that, did you, greg?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Good, so he didn't hear that last bit.

jokes.text: Murray to Bret: 'Have you thought about these rappers that you're dissing? You might hurt their feelings.' Then breaks into song.
jokes.setup: Murray is warning Bret about the dangerous consequences of dissing rappers
jokes.punchline: His concern isn't their violent retaliation but their hurt feelings — and this absurd pivot triggers a full musical number
jokes.explanation: Effective pivot that sets up the song. The joke is the complete inversion of expected rap-feud stakes: not bodily harm but feelings. Functions as a perfect song introduction.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's how you diss someone. Did you see what I did there? I didn't start a fight with greg, I didn't hurt his feelings. Greg's got feelings.

jokes.text: 'Hurt Feelings' song — opening verses: 'Some people say that rappers don't have feelings / We have feelings / Some people say that we are not rappers / We're rappers / That hurts our feelings'
jokes.setup: The song opens with declarations about rappers having feelings
jokes.punchline: The meta-complaint 'some people say that we are not rappers / that hurts our feelings' — their credibility as rappers is itself questioned within the rap about their feelings
jokes.explanation: The song's opening gambit is self-undermining in the best way. 'We're vincible' is a perfect coined word — the opposite of 'invincible,' used with complete confidence. The meta-complaint about not being recognized as rappers, within the rap, is clever layering.

dialogue.line: some people say that rappers don't have feelings
we have feelings

jokes.text: 'I feel like a prize asshole / No one even mentions my casserole'
jokes.setup: Verse about cooking a meal for friends and no one complimenting it
jokes.punchline: 'I feel like a prize asshole / No one even mentions my casserole' — the rhyme of 'asshole/casserole' and the specific grievance (uncomplimenting a casserole) is the joke
jokes.explanation: The asshole/casserole rhyme is an all-timer. The specific choice of 'casserole' as the grievance — so domestic, so un-gangster — is perfectly calibrated. The rhyme itself is surprising and satisfying. One of the most quotable lines in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: we're vincible

jokes.text: 'You could've said something nice about my profiteroles'
jokes.setup: Rapper laments no one praised his casserole
jokes.punchline: Escalates to profiteroles — an even more unlikely gangster grievance, and a French pastry at that
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation from casserole to profiteroles — the Frenchness of 'profiteroles' adds another layer of incongruity. A gangster rapper complaining about unrecognized profiteroles is inherently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I feel like a prize asshole, no one even mentions my casserole

jokes.text: Wetsuit verse: 'I was shopping for a wetsuit to scuba dive / but every suit I tried was too big around the thighs / and the assistant suggested I try a ladies' size / I'm not gonna wear a ladies' wetsuit, I'm a man / Get me a small man's wetsuit, please'
jokes.setup: Verse about a humiliating shopping experience
jokes.punchline: 'I'm not gonna wear a ladies' wetsuit, I'm a man / Get me a small man's wetsuit, please' — the plaintive 'please' at the end, and the specificity of 'small man's wetsuit,' is the joke
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'too big around the thighs' and 'small man's wetsuit' is superb. This reads as genuinely autobiographical (possibly is) which makes it funnier. The polite, deflated 'please' at the end of a rap lyric is a perfect tonal subversion. Very strong character integration — this is Bret's insecurity made musical.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: you could've said something nice about my profita rolls

jokes.text: Birthday verse: 'It's my birthday, 2003 / Waiting for a call from my family / They forgot about me' / 'The day after my birthday is not my birthday, mom'
jokes.setup: Rapper's family forgot his birthday
jokes.punchline: 'The day after my birthday is not my birthday, mom' — the exasperated, childlike specificity of having to explain this concept to one's mother
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '2003' grounds it hilariously. But the punchline — 'The day after my birthday is not my birthday, mom' — is the standout. It's the kind of sentence only a genuinely wounded person would need to articulate, and that earnestness is what makes it so funny and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: and the assistant suggested I try a ladies' size

jokes.text: Friends-in-town verse: 'I call my friends, say let's go into town / but they're all too busy to go into town / So I go by myself, I go into town / Then I see all my friends, they're all in town / They're all lined up to watch that movie Maid in Manhattan'
jokes.setup: Friends claim to be too busy to go into town
jokes.punchline: They're all in town anyway — watching 'Maid in Manhattan,' which is the specific detail that makes the betrayal even more pathetic
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'into town' is rhythmically hypnotic and funny. The slow build — he goes alone, sees them all there — is perfectly structured. 'Maid in Manhattan' is the devastating specific detail: they'd rather see a Jennifer Lopez romcom than hang out with you. Excellent economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm not gonna wear a ladies' wetsuit, I'm a man

jokes.text: Rapid-fire questions verse: 'Have you ever been told that your ass is too big? / Have you ever been asked if your hair is a wig? / Have you ever been told you're mediocre in bed? / Have you ever been told you've got a weird-shaped head?'
jokes.setup: Call-and-response questions about personal humiliations
jokes.punchline: Each question is more specific and personal than the last, escalating from physical insults to 'mediocre in bed' to 'weird-shaped head'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation sequence. 'Mediocre in bed' is particularly good — not catastrophically bad, just middling, which is somehow more humiliating. 'Weird-shaped head' is a classic FOTC specificity move.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: get me a small man's wetsuit, please

jokes.text: 'Has your family ever forgotten you and driven away? / Once again, they forgot about J'
jokes.setup: Escalating list of humiliations
jokes.punchline: 'Once again, they forgot about J' — 'once again' implies this is a pattern, not an isolated incident
jokes.explanation: 'Once again' is doing tremendous work. The family abandonment is a callback to the birthday verse, and 'once again' suggests chronic neglect. The rapper abbreviating himself to 'J' while describing being literally abandoned by his family is a perfect tonal mix of bravado and pathos.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: they forgot about me

jokes.text: 'Were you ever called homo because at school you took Drama? / Have you ever been told that you look like a llama?'
jokes.setup: Continuing list of humiliations
jokes.punchline: 'Look like a llama' — the specificity of llama, the drama/llama near-rhyme scheme, and the escalation from social cruelty to animal comparison
jokes.explanation: The llama comparison is perfectly chosen — not a conventionally insulting animal, just a weird one with an odd face. 'Drama/llama' rhyme scheme is fun. The Drama class homophobia is a knowing wink at the performers' own theatrical backgrounds.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: the day after my birthday is not my birthday, mom

jokes.text: 'Tears of a rapper / I'm crying tears of a rapper' — song outro with both performers
jokes.setup: Song has catalogued extensive humiliations
jokes.punchline: 'Tears of a rapper' as the closing refrain — presenting their weeping as a distinct genre of tears worthy of documentation
jokes.explanation: Functional outro that lands without being exceptional. 'Tears of a rapper' as a concept — implying these tears are categorically different — is a nice bit of absurd self-seriousness.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: they're all lined up to watch that movie 'maid in manhattan'

jokes.text: Murray after the song: 'Yes, well, all good examples.' Then continues warning about rappers hurting Bret's 'pretty little brain.'
jokes.setup: A full musical number just occurred detailing emotional humiliations
jokes.punchline: Murray responds as if they were literally useful supporting examples in a meeting presentation: 'All good examples'
jokes.explanation: Perfect post-song deflation. Murray treats the entire musical number as a PowerPoint slide, validating it bureaucratically. 'All good examples' is Murray at his most oblivious-manager best.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: they're all lined up to watch that movie 'maid in Manhattan'

jokes.text: Murray's escalating warning about rappers: 'You hurt their feelings and kaboom... they'll hurt you more than your feelings... they'll hurt your pretty little brain, Bret. It'll be gone, then you'll have no feelings.'
jokes.setup: Murray warning Bret about consequences of dissing rappers
jokes.punchline: The logic spiral: hurt feelings → hurt brain → no feelings. Murray trying to sound threatening but producing only confused circular reasoning
jokes.explanation: Murray attempts menace and produces only bafflement. 'Pretty little brain' is an odd, slightly maternal turn of phrase. The logic collapses on itself entertainingly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: once again, they forgot about j

jokes.text: Murray: 'Here in the United States, rivalries between rappers usually result in death or worse.' Bret: 'Death?' Murray: 'Yeah, or worse.'
jokes.setup: Murray warns about rapper rivalries resulting in 'death or worse'
jokes.punchline: When Bret questions 'death?', Murray confirms it — but doesn't clarify what could be worse than death, leaving the 'worse' hanging as an unknown
jokes.explanation: Classic deadpan absurdist construct. The phrase 'death or worse' implies something worse than death exists, and Murray's straight-faced confirmation without elaboration is perfect. Maximum comedy per syllable.

dialogue.line: were you ever called 'homo' because at school you took Drama?

jokes.text: Bret: 'Isn't it the same way with rappers back in Newfoundland?' Jemaine: 'New Zealand.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks about rapper culture back home
jokes.punchline: He says 'Newfoundland' instead of New Zealand; Jemaine's flat one-word correction has the weariness of someone who corrects this constantly
jokes.explanation: A quick, efficient joke about American ignorance of New Zealand, but interestingly it's Bret (a New Zealander) who makes the error. Jemaine's weary 'New Zealand.' does good work in one word.

dialogue.line: have you ever been told that you look like a llama?

jokes.text: Murray's urban legend about rappers: 'I heard of one rapper... he chopped this guy's whole body off, just left the dick behind.' Bret: 'Don't you mean they chopped off his dick?' Murray: 'No, I mean they held his dick and chopped his whole body off. That's all he was in the end... a dick.'
jokes.setup: Murray tells a cautionary tale about rapper violence
jokes.punchline: The inversion — not removing the dick from the body, but removing the body from the dick — and the philosophical conclusion 'That's all he was in the end... a dick'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes, possibly one of the show's best. The inversion of the expected mutilation story (body removed FROM the dick rather than dick removed from body) is a genuinely original comedic concept. Murray delivers it with complete earnestness. The philosophical kicker 'That's all he was in the end... a dick' works as both literal description of the scenario and as a moral judgment. Dark, surprising, brilliantly structured, and utterly quotable. Justifies 8+ easily — comparable in structure and surprise to the vasectomy anchor joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Death?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, or worse. Isn't it the same way with rappers back in newfoundland?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm not sure.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: New zealand.

jokes.text: Johnnyboy's gang resumé: 'Do I have gang experience? I was in the snake eyes, the duckies, the cuckoos, the vampires, the pharaohs, the ballroom dancers, the hobo lords, the little enchanters...' then 'I was a navy seal for 15 years. I'm officially AWOL, so don't ask them about my existence.'
jokes.setup: Bret holds gang tryouts and asks about experience
jokes.punchline: The list of gang names escalates from tough-sounding to increasingly absurd ('ballroom dancers,' 'little enchanters'), then the Navy SEAL revelation is delivered with complete paranoid sincerity
jokes.explanation: The gang name list is a masterclass in comedic escalation. It starts realistically ('snake eyes') and slides into the absurd ('ballroom dancers,' 'little enchanters'). The Navy SEAL non-sequitur with the 'don't ask them about my existence' paranoia is an excellent payoff. The list structure is well-paced.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I heard of one rapper... he chopped this guy's whole body off, just left the dick behind.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Don't you mean they... they chopped off his dick?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, I mean they held his dick and chopped his whole body off. That's all he was in the end... a dick.

jokes.text: Mr. and Mrs. Li's reason for wanting to join the gang: 'We run the internet cafe downstairs. Vandals graffiti our store window. We want to stop them.' Followed by 'He says you have a kind face.'
jokes.setup: Bret's gang tryouts — asking why candidates want to join
jokes.punchline: The Lis want gang membership purely as a practical solution to a shopkeeping problem; 'He says you have a kind face' is a disarming non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of gang-movie tropes. The Lis approach gang membership as a practical consumer decision — 'we have a problem, you seem nice, let's do business.' 'He says you have a kind face' as the final reason is a lovely non-sequitur.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Do I have gang experience? I was in the snake eyes, the duckies, the cuckoos, the vampires, the pharaohs, the ballroom dancers, the hobo lords, the little enchanters...

jokes.text: Dave's special skills: 'I can make a weapon out of pretty much anything that's lying around. Like when I'm gardening, some a-hole tries to sneak up and take me out... watering can tied to a hose. Pop! Cops show up, what's that, officer? No, just watering the geraniums.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks about special skills for the gang
jokes.punchline: Dave's survival scenario involves gardening, a watering can weapon, and then seamlessly returning to horticultural normalcy while the cops watch
jokes.explanation: Classic Dave: hyper-specific tactical thinking applied to completely mundane scenarios. The geraniums detail is perfect — not just any flowers, geraniums specifically. Good character-specific comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I was a navy seal for 15 years.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I'm officially awol, so don't ask them about my existence, 'cause they'll deny it.

jokes.text: Dave's extended gang resumé continues: 'And in '54 I joined the monkey lords. After that, the rockers, the gentleman jims, the tsars, the stars, the might midgets from Queens.' Bret: 'So you've had a lot of gang experience, basically.' Dave: 'They're all gangs, big daddy!'
jokes.setup: Dave continues listing his gang affiliations
jokes.punchline: 'The might midgets from Queens' is the absurd peak of the list; Bret's masterfully understated summary 'So you've had a lot of gang experience, basically'; Dave's 'They're all gangs, big daddy!'
jokes.explanation: Mirrors Johnnyboy's list with Dave's own roster. 'The might midgets from Queens' is a great gang name. 'They're all gangs, big daddy!' — Dave's defensive pride — is a nice button.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Li
dialogue.line: He says you have a kind face.

jokes.text: Murray meets Australians in the lift: 'Are you a kiwi?' / 'Yes, hello.' / 'Oh no. A kiwi?' / 'Oh no.' / 'Uh-oh. Australian. Uh-oh.'
jokes.setup: Murray gets in a lift and is recognized as a New Zealander
jokes.punchline: Both parties perform symmetric dismay at each other's nationality — both say 'Oh no' and 'Uh-oh' — revealing that the New Zealand/Australia rivalry is mutual and equally petty
jokes.explanation: Good comedic economy in the symmetric dismay. The NZ/Australia rivalry is well-worn territory but the mutual 'uh-oh' is a nice structural detail. Sets up the Australian consulate running gag.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: watering can tied to a hose. Pop! Cops show up, 'what's that, officer? No, just watering the geraniums.'

jokes.text: 'Just pulling your tit.' / 'Yeah, just pulling your boob.' — Australians apologize for the mineral exports joke using increasingly odd euphemisms for 'pulling your leg'
jokes.setup: Australians tease Murray then apologize
jokes.punchline: 'Just pulling your tit' then 'Just pulling your boob' — escalating from leg to increasingly inappropriate body parts as euphemisms
jokes.explanation: Nice little joke about Australian vernacular being pushed beyond its limits. 'Pulling your leg' → 'pulling your tit' → 'pulling your boob' is a clean escalation. Quotable and efficiently delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Australian
dialogue.line: Oh no. A kiwi?

dialogue.speaker: Kiwi
dialogue.line: Oh no. Uh-oh.

dialogue.speaker: Australian
dialogue.line: Australian. Uh-oh.

jokes.text: Dave at gang defense meeting: 'I'm available most days after 6:00 but not weekends... and I can't say why.' Murray: 'Dinner with your parents?' Dave: 'Huh! No.'
jokes.setup: Dave announces he can't do weekends but won't explain why
jokes.punchline: Murray's guess of 'dinner with your parents' — which Dave dismisses with wounded indignation — implies it's probably exactly that
jokes.explanation: Quick efficient joke. Dave's tough-guy persona is undermined by the perfectly plausible guess. His excessive 'Huh! No.' protests too much.

dialogue.speaker: Australian
dialogue.line: Murray, I think you dropped new zealand's mineral exports for '08-'09.

jokes.text: Bret's gang schedule offer: 'I'm available all the time, but I like to go to bed by 8:00 at night so I can build up my Zs for the next day. Otherwise I get real tired. Oh, and I got to take toilet breaks. I have a bladder thing.'
jokes.setup: Bret, the gang leader, announces his scheduling constraints
jokes.punchline: 8pm bedtime plus a bladder condition — not exactly menacing gang-leader qualities
jokes.explanation: Beautifully anti-gangster. The gang leader's primary logistical constraints are his bedtime and bladder. 'Build up my Zs' is an endearingly dorky phrase. Strong character integration — Bret's meekness constantly undermining his aspirations.

dialogue.line: Just pulling your tit.
Yeah, just pulling your boob.

jokes.text: Gang member shows off handmade item: 'If anyone wants me to make them a pair of these bad boys, I'll need about a week's notice.' — visual beat of showing whatever craft project he's made
jokes.setup: Wrapping up gang scheduling meeting, Johnnyboy adds a note
jokes.punchline: He's apparently been knitting or crafting something during the gang meeting and is offering his services — the 'bad boys' are presumably some domestic craft item
jokes.explanation: Visual gag that works better on screen. The tough gang veteran offering handcrafted goods with a week's lead time is a nice absurdist character beat. Rewatch bonus for the visual.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I'm available most days after 6:00 but not weekends... and I can't say why.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Dinner with your parents?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Huh! No.

jokes.text: Mel presents fan art of Jemaine to him at his door. Jemaine: 'What is it?' Mel: 'You don't like it.' Jemaine: 'I do.' Mel: 'No, you don't. You practically admitted that you don't like it.' Jemaine: 'I... did I?'
jokes.setup: Mel gives Jemaine fan art she painted of him
jokes.punchline: She interprets 'What is it?' as an admission he doesn't like it, then escalates her distress while he scrambles to backtrack — trapped in an inescapable logic loop
jokes.explanation: Classic Mel cringe comedy. Her hypersensitivity traps Jemaine before he's even said anything wrong. The escalation from 'You don't like it' to 'You practically admitted' in seconds is well-paced. Strong Mel character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I have a bladder thing.

jokes.text: Mel's dramatic swing to joy: 'You love it! I can't believe he loves it! Oh God, you should hang it on your wall so you can see how strong and powerful you are, like the God Ra... the sun God Ra... or like a pharaoh.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine grudgingly says he likes the painting
jokes.punchline: Mel's instant swing from devastation to ecstatic hyperbole — comparing Jemaine to Egyptian sun gods — reveals the painting is an idealized muscular warrior version of him
jokes.explanation: Mel's emotional range from zero to hundred in seconds is her defining character trait. The painting being a warrior-pharaoh version of Jemaine (who is not muscular) is revealed here. 'Like the God Ra... the sun God Ra' — the clarification that Ra is a sun god makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You don't like it. No, you don't. You practically admitted that you don't like it.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'I'm not as muscley as that.' Mel: 'Yeah, that's what I said.' Doug (quietly): 'Be quiet, Doug.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine notes the painting is more muscular than he is
jokes.punchline: Mel agrees ('that's what I said'), inadvertently confirming she painted a better-looking version of him; Doug is pre-emptively silenced
jokes.explanation: Three beats in quick succession. Mel's unselfconscious confirmation that the painting is better-looking than Jemaine, then Doug being silenced before he can say anything, is economically funny.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: so you can see how strong and powerful you are, like the God ra... the sun God ra... or like a pharaoh.

jokes.text: Mel: 'Anyways, I made one of Bret, but it came out a little weird, so don't tell him, okay? It came out x-rated, actually... some shadowing on the crotch got out of hand.'
jokes.setup: Mel has painted both band members
jokes.punchline: The Bret painting came out X-rated due to 'shadowing on the crotch getting out of hand' — delivered as a completely casual artistic mishap
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Mel joke. 'Some shadowing on the crotch got out of hand' is the episode's funniest line about Mel — she treats the accidental creation of X-rated fan art as a minor artistic inconvenience. The euphemism 'got out of hand' is perfectly chosen. Strong character integration; only Mel would say this with zero embarrassment.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: I made one of Bret, but it came out a little weird, so don't tell him, okay? It came out x-rated, actually... some shadowing on the crotch got out of hand

jokes.text: Bret's gang mistakes Jemaine for an enemy: 'Bret! Bret! Bret! We got him good!' then 'False alarm. It's Jemaine.' followed by Dave attacking anyway: 'Can attack!'
jokes.setup: Gang is on high alert waiting for enemies to arrive
jokes.punchline: They attack Jemaine (Bret's own flatmate) by mistake; even after the false alarm is called, Dave still hits him ('Can attack!')
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy beat. 'False alarm' being immediately followed by 'Can attack!' suggests Dave operates on his own tactical logic regardless of instructions. The rewatch bonus is for the physical execution of the attack on Jemaine.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: We were just expecting missy Elliot was gonna come and chop my body off.

jokes.text: Jemaine asks Bret about his day. Bret: 'How was your day?' Jemaine: 'Uh, it was good.' Bret: 'I've been in the ceiling most of the day.'
jokes.setup: Normal conversational exchange about how the day went
jokes.punchline: Bret has spent the day in the ceiling — delivered as a completely unremarkable detail in polite conversation
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan. The extreme incongruity of 'I've been in the ceiling most of the day' delivered as casual small talk — with no elaboration and no reaction from Jemaine — is a textbook FOTC absurdist beat. Economical to a fault.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: This gang is disbandoned.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No. Dis... what?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Disbandoned.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Disbanded you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Abandoned? Yup, all of those things.

jokes.text: Murray's confusion: 'Right, Bret, what's this I hear from one of the band about you starting up a gang?' — Bret: 'You told me to start up a gang.' Murray: 'I said rappers have gangs and you shouldn't hassle rappers.' Bret: 'No, you said rappers have gangs, so I should start a gang.' Murray: 'Typical... you didn't listen.'
jokes.setup: Murray confronts Bret about the gang
jokes.punchline: Murray says 'typical, you didn't listen' — when actually Bret's interpretation ('I should start a gang') is a plausible reading of Murray's original warning
jokes.explanation: Good Murray moment. He accuses Bret of not listening when in fact the miscommunication is attributable to his own garbled warning. Murray's certainty in his own correctness is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes. Who was in your gang, you and your mom?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, Jemaine. It was me, my brother graeme, and my dad Gordon. Mom wasn't even allowed in it. No one knew why.

jokes.text: Murray: 'This gang is disbandoned.' Bret: 'No. Dis... what?' Murray: 'Disbandoned.' Jemaine: 'Disbanded?' Murray: 'Abandoned?' Murray: 'Yup, all of those things.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to formally dissolve Bret's gang
jokes.punchline: 'Disbandoned' — a portmanteau of 'disbanded' and 'abandoned,' which Murray defends by accepting both component words as equally valid
jokes.explanation: A wonderful Murray malapropism. 'Disbandoned' is a perfect blend word — it sounds exactly like something Murray would say with complete authority. His response when challenged — accepting both 'disbanded' and 'abandoned' as equivalent — shows Murray's strategy of doubling down rather than correcting. High quotability, strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Dad was the leader, graeme found nice places for us to ride to and I was the guy that looked after the bags.

jokes.text: Murray's yes/no loop with Bret: 'Yes. / No. / Yes! / No. / How about yes? / How about no? / Well, I'd rather you say yes. / No.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to get Bret to agree to disband the gang
jokes.punchline: The negotiation devolves into pure monosyllabic repetition, with Murray trying the negotiating tactic 'How about yes?' and offering 'I'd rather you say yes' as if it's a compelling argument
jokes.explanation: The escalation to pure yes/no trading is funny, but the killer detail is 'I'd rather you say yes' — Murray deploying his feelings as a negotiating tool. Economical and rhythmically satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I had to throw it away! Because Bret was jealous.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Really? Was he going apeshit?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He went apeshit.

jokes.text: Murray completes the pivot back: 'Nice guy, isn't he... Greg?' — after previously dissing Greg constantly, including seconds ago
jokes.setup: Murray has been secretly disparaging Greg all episode
jokes.punchline: Now that Greg has secured him a plus-two, Murray is warmly complimentary: 'Nice guy, isn't he... Greg?'
jokes.explanation: Satisfying callback to the Greg-dissing thread. Murray's complete reversal based purely on the plus-two is well-observed character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: I swear on Doug's life I will fix this rift.

jokes.text: Murray reveals he used to be in a gang: 'It was me, my brother Graeme, and my dad Gordon. Mom wasn't even allowed in it. No one knew why.'
jokes.setup: Murray claims moral authority on gangs because he was in one
jokes.punchline: His gang was him, his brother, and his dad — a family bike gang — and his mom was excluded for unknown reasons
jokes.explanation: Superb Murray backstory joke. The gang being a dad-led family bicycle group is already funny; 'Mom wasn't even allowed in it. No one knew why' is the perfect detail — the arbitrary exclusion, the family mystery, the child Murray apparently compliant with Dad's gang rules. Very Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Australian
dialogue.line: Miss new zealand... best looking Sheila in the whole country. Sorry, I didn't recognize you without the crown and sash.

jokes.text: Murray describes his gang role: 'Dad was the leader, Graeme found nice places for us to ride to, and I was the guy that looked after the bags.'
jokes.setup: Murray describes the hierarchy of his biker gang
jokes.punchline: Murray's role was... bag-keeper. His brother found 'nice places' for rides. His dad led. Murray looked after the bags.
jokes.explanation: Beautiful escalation on the family gang joke. The roles are hilariously mundane: finding 'nice places,' keeping bags. Murray's gang role — not navigator, not enforcer, not even the one who finds nice places — is bag-keeper. It's a perfect degradation that's also unmistakably Murray. One of the episode's strongest character comedy moments.

dialogue.speaker: Ambassador
dialogue.line: But I thought flossy the sheep won miss new zealand every year.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Murray, is there anything band-related in today's meeting?' Murray: 'Yes, there is, actually, but I haven't finished my gang stories.'
jokes.setup: Murray has spent an entire meeting on non-band topics
jokes.punchline: He acknowledges there ARE band-related items but refuses to get to them because his gang reminiscences take priority
jokes.explanation: Good Murray inefficiency joke. The acknowledgment that band business exists but is being actively deprioritized for gang nostalgia is perfectly Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: have you ever been told that you're miss new zealand?

jokes.text: Murray's final agenda item: 'Bret, Murray, curry. Any use to you?' — offering another rhyming word set as they leave
jokes.setup: Meeting is ending chaotically
jokes.punchline: Murray's parting shot is the Texas/Lexus bit again, now with 'Bret/Murray/curry' — shouting rhyming words at retreating band members
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the Texas/Lexus bit. Murray's dedication to the rhyming-words project even as the meeting collapses is funny. 'Bret, Murray, curry' — including his own name in the rhyme set — is a lovely absurdist touch.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Mos def, the guy from 'law & order: SUV'? No, that's ice cube, but keep an eye out for him.

jokes.text: Mel confronts Jemaine about painting being on the stoop. Jemaine: 'I had to throw it away!' Mel: 'Why?' Jemaine: 'Because Bret was jealous.' Mel: 'Really? Was he going apeshit?' Jemaine: 'He went apeshit.'
jokes.setup: Mel finds her painting by the trash; Jemaine tries to explain
jokes.punchline: Jemaine lies about Bret being jealous; Mel's intense investment in the fiction ('Was he going apeshit?') and Jemaine's immediate amplification ('He went apeshit') creates a mutual fabrication
jokes.explanation: The Mel-Jemaine dynamic at its best. Mel's enthusiastic adoption of 'going apeshit' as a phrase, and her delight at the idea of Bret's jealousy, reveals her obsessiveness. Jemaine's weak lie spiraling into 'he went apeshit' is a nice comedic escalation driven by character.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: he was the voice of lightning the racehorse.

jokes.text: Mel: 'I swear on Doug's life I will fix this rift.'
jokes.setup: Mel commits to resolving the (fictional) jealousy between Bret and Jemaine
jokes.punchline: She swears on Doug's life — not her own — treating her husband's existence as a disposable oath object
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and character integration. The joke is entirely in 'Doug's life' — four words that tell you everything about how Mel regards her husband relative to her obsession with the band. She doesn't swear on her own life; Doug's is more expendable. Could only be Mel. Justifies the high score.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, cool your jets, don't sweat

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: don't flip, don't get upset

jokes.text: Mel insists on standing uncomfortably close to Jemaine: 'Jemaine, stay close.' 'I am close. You're too close now.' 'No, but... closer. Put your feet next to mine... that's it. We're together, all right?'
jokes.setup: Mel escorts Jemaine to the consulate party
jokes.punchline: Her definition of 'together' involves feet touching; Jemaine's increasingly alarmed objections
jokes.explanation: Good cringe beat. Mel's escalating physical proximity requirements are a nice expression of her obsessiveness. Stronger visually than in transcript form.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: not gonna sleep till I found him I'll pound him, I'll bet he regrets he ever messed with Bret from the tough brets!

jokes.text: Mel at the party: 'You said there was gonna be cheese. You said there was gonna be free cheese.' Murray: 'There will be cheese. Over there. Look.' Mel: 'Right, let's go and get some cheese together, okay? All right, go. No, you're too close.' — the physical proximity joke inverted
jokes.setup: Mel was demanding closeness from Jemaine; now Murray is apparently too close
jokes.punchline: The proximity complaint inverted — she was insisting on closeness with Jemaine, now she tells Murray he's 'too close'
jokes.explanation: Functional callback. The inversion of the proximity joke works but isn't as sharp as the original.

dialogue.line: what's the hurry?
hello, Murray, glad I caught ya
who's this? Your daughter?

jokes.text: Australian diplomat at the party: 'Who's this, Murray... your wife? No, his wife... his wife left him.' Murray: 'Jemaine!' — Jemaine has just told everyone Murray's marriage failed
jokes.setup: Murray is greeted by the Australian diplomats he met in the elevator
jokes.punchline: Jemaine immediately and helpfully informs everyone that Murray's wife left him, when asked if Mel was Murray's wife
jokes.explanation: Good cringe beat. Jemaine volunteering Murray's divorce as relevant information — completely socially unaware — is well-played. Murray's wounded 'Jemaine!' is the button.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: You could get 10 years for that.

jokes.text: Australian diplomat: 'Miss New Zealand... best looking Sheila in the whole country. Sorry, I didn't recognize you without the crown and sash.' — referring to Jemaine (a man) as Miss New Zealand
jokes.setup: Australians are ribbing Murray's friend
jokes.punchline: They suggest Jemaine — who is a man — must be Miss New Zealand, implying he's the best-looking woman in the country
jokes.explanation: Standard beauty-pageant-for-a-man joke, elevated slightly by Jemaine's specific deadpan reaction and the Australian-NZ rivalry context. Sets up the ambassador payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Where's the dapper dukes' hideout? It was right here. You know, we laid low for three straight months back in '58.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Let's just get all their cheese and grapes and get out of here.' — retaliation plan is stealing the party food
jokes.setup: Murray is humiliated and wants to respond
jokes.punchline: His revenge is maximum food acquisition — 'all their cheese and grapes'
jokes.explanation: Murray's petty revenge consisting entirely of cheese and grape confiscation is a very Murray plan. The specificity of 'grapes' alongside cheese is funnier than just cheese alone.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Maybe you're right, lil Louie. I'm getting old. This game ain't no good for me no more. I'm sorry, Louie. I'm out. So long, lil Louie.

jokes.text: Ambassador, after apologizing: 'But I thought Flossy the sheep won Miss New Zealand every year.' — the Australian Ambassador himself makes a sheep joke
jokes.setup: The Australian Ambassador has just apologized for his staff's behavior and promised to take it seriously
jokes.punchline: He immediately makes a worse sheep-shagger joke himself — the person Murray went to for justice is worse than the offenders
jokes.explanation: Well-structured misdirection. The setup — Murray escalates to the Ambassador for justice — creates an expectation of vindication. The subversion is that the most senior Australian official is the worst offender. 'Flossy the sheep' is a specific enough detail to make the joke land. Good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: How come I'm wearing gumboots?

jokes.text: Murray: 'Greg, what are you laughing for?' — Greg (presumably on the intercom) has been laughing at the sheep joke
jokes.setup: Murray has just been publicly humiliated by the Australian Ambassador
jokes.punchline: Greg, miles away on the intercom, is apparently laughing at Murray's humiliation
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the broken intercom thread. Greg — who has been quietly absorbing Murray's disses all year — gets his moment of schadenfreude, apparently tuned into whatever is happening and laughing. Murray's outraged 'Greg, what are you laughing for?' is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Well, in the future, I suggest you don't kick children. we'll let this go because we don't like that particular kid, but we've got our eyes on you.

jokes.text: Reprise of 'Hurt Feelings' instrumental — Murray walking alone, dejected, through the party
jokes.setup: Murray has just been publicly humiliated by the Australian Ambassador
jokes.punchline: The 'Hurt Feelings' melody plays as Murray walks dejectedly — the song's theme now applies to Murray himself, making it meta-commentary on his situation
jokes.explanation: Elegant structural joke. The episode's central song about hurt feelings now becomes Murray's theme as he wanders humiliated. It's a callback, a visual gag, and meta-commentary all in one. The show's musical form being used to comment on the character situation is a sophisticated comedy move that rewards rewatching.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: As you can see, I pretty much got the handsomeness. That's Jemaine. If you don't believe it, he can take his shirt off and you can be sure about it.

jokes.text: Mel offers to pose for Jemaine's portrait; Jemaine declines without hesitation. Doug: 'Mel, I could pose for you.' Mel: 'Oh, no thanks.' — Doug shut down instantly
jokes.setup: Mel wants Jemaine to paint her; the painting situation lingers
jokes.punchline: Doug offering to pose and being immediately dismissed — Mel doesn't even need a moment to consider
jokes.explanation: Efficient character comedy. Doug's gentle attempt to be included in the creative process is immediately crushed. 'Oh, no thanks' — Mel doesn't even have to think about it. Doug's marginalization is a consistent background joke of the show.

jokes.text: Song reprise verse: 'Have you ever been dissed over the intercom? / Have you ever found a gift you've given thrown away? / Have you ever been told that you're Miss New Zealand?'
jokes.setup: The 'Hurt Feelings' song returns with new verses
jokes.punchline: The new verses directly reference events from THIS episode — the intercom dissing, the thrown-away painting, the Miss NZ insult — making the song a recap of the episode's humiliations
jokes.explanation: Structurally smart. The show uses the song reprise to tie together the episode's comedic threads — the broken intercom, Mel's art, the Australian diplomats — into a meta-summary sung as if they're generic rap grievances. 'Have you ever been dissed over the intercom?' is specific enough to be funny while fitting perfectly into the song's rhetorical framework. Strong episode-level craft.

jokes.text: Post-party small talk: 'Thanks for bringing me to the party, Murray.' 'That's all right. Good cheese, wasn't it?'
jokes.setup: They've just returned from a deeply humiliating evening
jokes.punchline: Murray's consolation is that the cheese was good — his petty cheese-acquisition plan was at least partially successful
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on the evening. Murray's silver lining is entirely cheese-based. 'Good cheese, wasn't it?' delivered with complete sincerity after a night of national humiliation is economical and very Murray.

jokes.text: Bret and Dave on watch: 'We're looking for, basically, all rappers.' Dave: 'Mos Def, all the guys.' Bret: 'Mos Def, the guy from Law & Order: SVU?' Dave: 'No, that's Ice Cube, but keep an eye out for him.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Dave are on lookout for enemy rappers
jokes.punchline: Bret confuses Mos Def (rapper) with Ice Cube (who was apparently on Law & Order: SVU in this fiction), and Dave corrects him with equal misinformation
jokes.explanation: Good escalating confusion sequence. The characters' complete inability to correctly identify any rapper, while being deadly serious about it, is a nice extension of the show's NZ-outsider perspective on American hip-hop culture.

jokes.text: Extended rapper-filmography confusion: Ice Cube was in 'Are We There Yet?' and 'Are We Done Yet?', Method Man from 'Meet the Spartans,' Snoop Dogg 'was the voice of Lightning the racehorse.' 'Yeah, you don't want him on your ass.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Dave try to establish which rappers are dangerous based on their movie credits
jokes.punchline: Their threat assessment is based entirely on film roles: Snoop Dogg's danger level is determined by him voicing a racehorse; Dave concludes 'You don't want him on your ass' — a horse-related double meaning
jokes.explanation: The rapper filmography discussion escalates beautifully. 'Lightning the racehorse' is a perfectly absurd detail (if it's even real). 'You don't want him on your ass' as both a threat assessment and an accidental horse-racing double entendre is strong. The logic of judging rapper danger level by movie résumé is genuinely original.

jokes.text: Bret confronts the teen graffiti artists: 'Well, on behalf of the tough Brets, I'd like you to stop graffitiing Mr. and Mrs. Li's internet shop.' Teens: 'Who are you?' / 'Yeah, get lost.'
jokes.setup: Bret has assembled his gang to confront dangerous vandals
jokes.punchline: His formal, polite speech ('on behalf of the tough Brets') is instantly dismissed by teenagers who tell him to get lost
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the song. Bret's polite gang-spokesperson mode ('I'd like you to stop graffitiing') is completely deflated by immediate dismissal from teenagers. The formality of 'on behalf of the Tough Brets' against the teens' casual contempt is well-calibrated.

jokes.text: 'Stay Cool, Bret' song — Jemaine and Murray trying to calm Bret, then pivoting to their own anxieties: 'Not gonna sleep till I found him / I'll pound him, I'll bet he regrets / He ever messed with Bret from the tough Brets!'
jokes.setup: Jemaine tries to calm Bret down with a song advising restraint
jokes.punchline: The 'stay cool' song escalates into its own aggressive revenge fantasy — the people telling Bret to stay cool are not staying cool themselves
jokes.explanation: The episode's best song. The conceit — a calm-down song that spirals into rage — is perfectly structured. Jemaine instructs Bret to stay cool while simultaneously promising to pound the kid until he regrets it. The contradiction is the joke, and it's executed with perfect musical escalation. 'He ever messed with Bret from the tough Brets' as the aggressive conclusion of a de-escalation song is a top-tier FOTC construction. Justifies 8.5+ — the structural subversion is comparable to the best moments of the show.

jokes.text: 'Stay Cool, Murray' — verse addressed to Murray: 'What's the hurry? / Hello, Murray, glad I caught ya / Who's this? Your daughter? / Your girlfriend looks great, do you want a date? / Just pulling your tit, mate...'
jokes.setup: The Stay Cool song expands to include Murray, who apparently encounters the Australians mid-song
jokes.punchline: The Australian diplomats' 'just pulling your tit' catchphrase is incorporated into the musical number, and Murray apparently needs to be told to stay cool as well
jokes.explanation: Smart integration of the episode's Australian subplot into the musical number. 'Just pulling your tit, mate' being sung in the context of the stay-cool song is a callback that earns its place. Murray needing his own 'stay cool' verse implies he's about to confront the Australians again.

jokes.text: Mid-song fight sound effects: 'Bam! / Pow!' interspersed with 'Stay / Cool' — the song literally represents a street fight in musical form
jokes.setup: The Stay Cool song has been escalating toward confrontation
jokes.punchline: Comic-book fight sounds ('Bam!' 'Pow!') set to music, while the refrain insists they're staying cool — the violence and the message are in direct contradiction, musicalized
jokes.explanation: The musical fight sequence is a brilliant use of the form. Having 'Bam!' and 'Pow!' as song lyrics while 'Stay cool' plays over the top is perfectly absurd. It's both a parody of '60s Batman sound effects and a commentary on the characters' inability to actually stay cool. Strong structural commitment.

jokes.text: Bret explains confrontation to teens: 'So you should respect other people's property.' Then accidentally kicks a kid. 'Bret, you just kicked a kid!' 'Sorry.' 'You could get 10 years for that.'
jokes.setup: Bret delivers a moral lecture to the teens about property
jokes.punchline: Mid-lecture, he kicks one of the kids. Then Dave's legal threat: '10 years.'
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation. Bret is doing his moral-authority speech and physically assaults a minor. Dave's immediate '10 years' assessment is perfectly deadpan. The accident-then-apology-then-legal-consequence structure works well.

jokes.text: Dave, fleeing the scene: 'Let's haul ass fast. I know a place we can hide till this blows over. Come on. You dig?' Bret: 'I think so.'
jokes.setup: They need to flee after Bret kicks the kid
jokes.punchline: Dave's 1950s/60s slang ('haul ass,' 'blows over,' 'you dig?') signals that his criminal experience is entirely from a different era; Bret's cautious 'I think so' is the button
jokes.explanation: Good Dave character moment. His old-school vernacular reveals his gang era. 'You dig?' as a genuine question — not ironically — shows Dave is a time-displaced figure. Bret's unsure 'I think so' is a nice button.

jokes.text: Dave leads them to a building that used to be 'the dapper dukes' hideout' in 1958 — now it's presumably gone or replaced. 'Where's the dapper dukes' hideout? It was right here. You know, we laid low for three straight months back in '58.' Bret: 'Johnny, I think they've probably all retired by now.'
jokes.setup: Dave leads the group to a safe house
jokes.punchline: The safe house is from 1958 and no longer exists; Bret has to gently inform Dave that the gang members have 'probably all retired'
jokes.explanation: One of Dave's best moments. The revelation that his entire gang knowledge is from the 1950s — hideouts, slang, tactics — is a late-episode payoff on his character. Bret's gentle 'they've probably all retired' manages to be both the obvious observation and oddly poignant. Strong character specificity.

jokes.text: Dave's retirement speech: 'I'm getting old. This game ain't no good for me no more. I'm sorry, Louie. I'm out. So long, lil Louie.' — addressed to Bret, whom he's calling 'lil Louie'
jokes.setup: Dave's 1950s hideout no longer exists
jokes.punchline: Dave delivers a full gangster-retirement speech to Bret, whom he addresses as 'lil Louie' — a completely different name — treating the moment with total dramatic sincerity
jokes.explanation: Superb Dave moment. He delivers a full cinematic gangster-retirement monologue, names Bret 'lil Louie' (a completely different name, uncommented upon), and exits with theatrical gravity. Dave fully inhabiting a 1950s gang film while standing in contemporary New York is perfectly absurd. 'So long, lil Louie' as a farewell to someone named Bret is quietly hilarious.

jokes.text: Bret brings home the X-rated Mel painting and Jemaine reacts: 'I found that on the steps downstairs. I thought you painted it.' Bret: 'How come I'm wearing gumboots?'
jokes.setup: The X-rated Bret painting (with crotch shadowing that 'got out of hand') has made its way home
jokes.punchline: Bret's only question about an apparently very explicit painting of himself is why he's wearing gumboots — the wrong detail to fixate on
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Given an apparently very explicit painting of himself, Bret's fixation is on the footwear. 'How come I'm wearing gumboots?' is maximally economical and maximally character-specific — Bret's oblivious practicality, his complete lack of vanity about the explicit content, his preoccupation with an irrelevant detail. Outstanding payoff on the painting setup from earlier.

jokes.text: Mel walks Jemaine's portrait around the block 'to get people's reactions.' Jemaine: trapped carrying a heroic nude-ish painting of himself around his neighborhood
jokes.setup: Mel wants to share the Jemaine portrait with the world
jokes.punchline: Jemaine is now obligated to walk around his neighborhood carrying a flattering idealized portrait of himself, with Mel narrating reactions
jokes.explanation: Good cringe visual. The embarrassment of being forced to tour your own flattering portrait is a nice escalation on the Mel plot. Stronger as a visual gag than on the page.

jokes.text: Police knock. Dave: 'Look, if my mom... I'm not going back to the big house, all right? I'm out of here. Stall 'em.' — flees at the sound of police
jokes.setup: Police arrive at the flat after the kid-kicking incident
jokes.punchline: Dave starts to say something about his mom, pivots to 'the big house,' and immediately flees — his reaction disproportionate even to his actual crime
jokes.explanation: Good Dave beat. 'If my mom...' aborted mid-sentence suggests an unresolved maternal legal situation. 'The big house' — again vintage crime slang — confirms his self-image as a career criminal. The mom reference is an intriguing dangling thread.

jokes.text: Police to Bret: 'We'll let this go because we don't like that particular kid, but we've got our eyes on you.'
jokes.setup: Police confront Bret about kicking a child
jokes.punchline: The police personally dislike the kid and are willing to let it slide for that reason — entirely unprofessional and absurdly matter-of-fact about it
jokes.explanation: Sharp absurdist joke. The police casually admitting personal bias against the victim as the reason for non-prosecution, delivered in official cop cadence, is excellent. 'We don't like that particular kid' as formal law enforcement reasoning is very funny.

jokes.text: Murray reveals the Lis have gone to Taiwan: 'They've gone to Taiwan to visit their son Phang. He just graduated dental school.' — casual epilogue delivered as if it's completely expected timing
jokes.setup: Bret asks where his gang members have gone
jokes.punchline: Murray knows an oddly specific amount of detail about the Li family's dental-school son, and the timing — during a gang crisis — is presented as a completely normal reason to leave
jokes.explanation: Functional absurdist beat. The specificity of 'Phang, dental school' is a nice detail. Murray knowing this is mildly funny. Not a standout.

jokes.text: Jemaine and Murray reveal they were hurt not to be asked: 'Someone whom you haven't asked. Who? Jemaine's talking about Jemaine, Bret. You could've asked him... or me... but you didn't.' Bret: 'Do you guys want to be in the gang?' Both: 'Yes! Yeah, I'd like to, yeah.'
jokes.setup: Bret is told his gang can have one more member
jokes.punchline: Both Jemaine and Murray were hurt at not being asked — despite having spent the entire episode objecting to the gang
jokes.explanation: Good callback and character moment. Both characters' genuine eagerness to join after spending the episode either objecting or ignoring the gang reveals their underlying desire to be included. 'Yes! Yeah, I'd like to, yeah' — their enthusiasm is touching and funny.

jokes.text: The gang immediately disbands again: Dave quits, so does Jemaine, then Murray: 'Well, if you're out and you're out, then I'm out. Really? Yeah, I'm out.' Bret: 'You're out as well? Well, I'm not leaving.' Bret alone: 'So that's it... I'm in. I'm the gang. I'm the Tough Brets.'
jokes.setup: The gang has just reformed with four members
jokes.punchline: All three members quit in rapid succession, leaving Bret alone as the entire gang
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode button on the gang plot. The gang forms and dissolves in about 30 seconds. Bret's dignified 'I'm the gang. I'm the Tough Brets.' — a one-person gang — is the ideal conclusion. The escalating defection sequence is well-paced.

jokes.text: Mel shows off the Jemaine portrait to strangers: 'This is a painting I did of Jemaine. As you can see, I pretty much got the handsomeness. That's Jemaine. If you don't believe it, he can take his shirt off and you can be sure about it.'
jokes.setup: Mel is parading the portrait around the neighborhood
jokes.punchline: 'He can take his shirt off and you can be sure about it' — Mel casually offering to strip Jemaine as proof of artistic accuracy to strangers
jokes.explanation: Mel at her most Mel. The escalation from 'I got the handsomeness' to offering to strip Jemaine as verification to strangers is perfectly unhinged. The casual authority with which she offers this — 'he can take his shirt off' — as if Jemaine has consented to this is very strong character comedy.

jokes.text: Mel: 'He is my muse and my joy. People don't appreciate art, do you? Unbelievable.' — delivered as an exasperated closing statement to strangers
jokes.setup: Mel presents the portrait to strangers who presumably don't react as she hopes
jokes.punchline: 'People don't appreciate art, do they? Unbelievable.' — Mel's self-righteous conclusion that the problem is everyone else's artistic ignorance
jokes.explanation: Good Mel button. Her certainty that unappreciative strangers are the problem — not the unsolicited exhibition of obsessive fan art — is perfectly in character. 'Unbelievable' as the final word is a nice touch.

tmdb_overview: Bret organizes a gang to protect himself from rappers he dissed in a song; Murray and Jemaine confront a group of Aussie bullies; Mel paints a portrait Jemaine can't get rid of.
